student_response: Ocean commerce was the buying and selling of goods across the ocean, and a consumer revolution was when people wanted to buy more things than before. Colonists took part in trade with merchants who crossed the ocean. Goods went on ships and then appeared in stores or were sold by local sellers. Some items were ordinary household objects, while other items were signs that a family had money. People could see what their neighbors owned and might want similar objects. Trade also involved farm products being sent away and finished products coming back. This happened over many years between 1650 and 1754. The amount of commerce was not always the same in every area. Some people bought many imported items and some people could afford very little. Ocean commerce and consumer buying were both important parts of colonial life during this period, and the colonies became involved in more buying, selling, shipping, and owning goods.

European nations had been building overseas empires since the age of exploration, and colonies were expected to trade with the mother country under mercantilism. This larger imperial system existed before and during the growth of British settlements in North America. Atlantic commerce included many kinds of exchanges during the colonial period. The Navigation Acts were part of this trade system. Triangular trade was another feature of Atlantic commerce. Sugar and molasses arrived from Caribbean plantations, while tea, cloth, and ceramics were imported from Britain. Merchants advertised goods in colonial newspapers, and some families bought imported objects on credit. The transatlantic slave trade was also part of the ocean economy, along with exports of tobacco and rice. These examples were present as buying became more common in the colonies. A consumer revolution describes the increasing appearance and purchase of goods in homes and shops. There were more objects available than earlier settlers had possessed. Atlantic commerce and the consumer revolution happened together in British North America from 1650 to 1754, with ships, merchants, stores, and customers all being seen in colonial society.

Political participation in the mainland colonies changed as participation became somewhat wider among people who met local qualifications, but the stronger continuity was that power remained limited to a narrow part of the population. In the early years, colonial politics was not open to everybody. Formal participation usually depended on a person's position and possessions. Later in the colonial period, more eligible residents were involved in choosing local officials and discussing public questions. Still, most residents had almost no formal political voice. The colonies had assemblies and local gatherings, although these bodies did not represent everyone equally. Political life could be active without being open to the whole population. Participation became somewhat wider for those who already qualified, while old boundaries continued to control who could take part. Public discussion existed, but actual authority stayed in relatively few hands. The basic pattern was limited expansion inside an unequal political system, rather than full participation for all colonial residents.

English colonists brought political customs from England, where Parliament and older ideas about the rights of Englishmen existed alongside the authority of the king. Colonies were also far across the Atlantic, and royal officials could not always supervise daily affairs closely, this was the broad setting for colonial government. Political participation took many forms from 1619 to 1754. The House of Burgesses first met in Virginia, and the Mayflower Compact was signed by settlers going to Plymouth. New England town meetings were held in local communities, and colonial assemblies existed in several colonies, these are examples of participation. Bacon's Rebellion involved western farmers and the Virginia government. The Zenger trial took place in New York and concerned a newspaper printer. The Great Awakening had traveling ministers and large religious gatherings, which were also public events. Voting rules often required property, women generally did not vote, enslaved people had no political rights, and many poor men were outside formal government. Governors, councils, assemblies, courts, and local meetings all existed at different points. The House of Burgesses was started by the Puritans in Virginia, while the Mayflower Compact created voting rights for every person in New England. Participation did not look the same in every colony and it did not stay exactly the same. There were elections and meetings, but there were also appointed governors and imperial authority, and colonists sometimes argued about taxes, land, religion, newspapers, or who had the right to make decisions. These political activities were present throughout the mainland colonies before 1754.

European settlement in North America took place while England was developing a stronger Parliament and traditions about the rights of English subjects. Colonists carried some of these traditions across the Atlantic, but they also lived far from the king and his officials. Political participation in the British mainland colonies expanded through elected assemblies and local government between 1619 and 1754, although it remained continuous in excluding women, enslaved people, Native people, and men without enough property. The House of Burgesses met in Virginia in 1619, it was an elected colonial assembly. The Mayflower Compact was signed in 1620, and town meetings were held in New England communities. Colonial governors and appointed councils were also part of government. Maryland had an Act of Toleration, and Pennsylvania was associated with Quaker government. Bacon's Rebellion happened in Virginia in 1676, it included backcountry settlers and the colonial leadership. The Salem witch trials involved courts and testimony. The Zenger trial in New York involved printing and criticism of a governor. The Great Awakening included George Whitefield and many large religious meetings. These events and institutions were all features of the colonial period. Voting usually had property qualifications, offices were generally held by men, and assemblies argued with governors over salaries and laws, there were also county courts and church meetings. In some places people voted directly at meetings, while in others representatives traveled to an assembly. The House of Burgesses was founded by William Penn before he established Pennsylvania, and the Puritans used it as their model for town meetings. By 1754, elections, petitions, assemblies, and public debates could be found in the colonies. At the same time, most inhabitants still had no formal vote. Political participation had grown for a section of colonial society, while the boundaries around political membership remained strong.

European exploration brought several empires into North America. England, France, Spain, and the Netherlands claimed land, built settlements, and entered existing Native trade networks. Indigenous nations already had their own political relations before permanent European colonies appeared. Diplomacy was used throughout the period from 1607 to 1754. The Powhatan Confederacy dealt with the settlers at Jamestown. Pocahontas and John Smith are connected to the early Virginia story. Later, the Pequot War and King Philip's War involved Native peoples and New England colonists. The Iroquois Confederacy was a major power in the northeast. The Covenant Chain was an agreement between the Iroquois and the French colonies. The Beaver Wars were another part of the region's history. Farther south, the Yamasee War took place near South Carolina. The Walking Purchase involved Pennsylvania and the Lenape. Conferences, wampum belts, trade, captives, interpreters, gifts, and treaties. Alliances with Europeans. Also fighting against them. Sometimes one nation had dealings with more than one colony. Other times a nation had a long agreement with a certain European power. The French traded in the interior and the English settled along the coast. Guns and metal goods appeared in trade. Missionaries also traveled among Native communities. A treaty could be discussed at a council and gifts could be exchanged. Colonial officials made promises about boundaries. Indigenous leaders visited colonial towns, and European representatives entered Native villages. These were diplomatic activities during the period. Not one single strategy in every place. Different nations, different colonies, and different moments from the founding of Jamestown to the years before the French and Indian War.

Before 1607, Indigenous nations maintained trade routes, rivalries, and alliances across North America, while European fishing and exploration had already introduced new goods and diseases. As permanent colonies expanded, Native nations continued using alliances and negotiation to protect their interests, but increasingly shifted toward balancing several European empires through formal trade and treaty networks. The Powhatan Confederacy negotiated with Jamestown and fought the English in the Anglo-Powhatan Wars, councils and force both appeared. In New England, the Wampanoag had an alliance with Plymouth, while Metacom later led King Philip's War. The Haudenosaunee maintained the Covenant Chain with New York and traded with English and French merchants, they occupied land between rival colonies. The Beaver Wars and mourning wars were part of Haudenosaunee policy. At Albany, treaty councils included speeches, gifts, wampum, and agreements. The Lenape had William Penn's treaties and then the Walking Purchase. Southern nations participated in the deerskin trade, and the Yamasee War involved South Carolina. Councils, alliances, trade, and armed conflict remained in use throughout the period. Alliances with different colonial powers also appeared. The later Treaty of Greenville involved Native nations and American officials. That agreement was from a later period. Indigenous diplomacy included war, trade, treaty meetings, gifts, and shifting alliances. Other treaty councils used interpreters, messengers, shared meals, ceremonial language, and promises. Native nations remained diplomatic actors, and their policies were not identical from one nation or decade to another.

European empires established colonies in the Americas after Atlantic exploration, and migration included both people who chose to move and people carried by force. By the late seventeenth century Britain controlled colonies with different climates, religions, labor systems, and settlement patterns. From 1680 to 1754, the middle colonies gained diversity mainly from voluntary ethnic and religious migration, while the southern colonies gained diversity more from plantation labor and forced African migration, producing greater religious pluralism in the middle region and a stricter racial hierarchy in the South. Pennsylvania was founded by William Penn and was associated with Quakers. Philadelphia became a large port. Germans settled in Pennsylvania, along with Scots-Irish migrants in frontier areas. New York had earlier Dutch residents, and New Jersey also contained several religious groups. The middle colonies grew wheat and were sometimes called the bread colonies. Churches included Quaker meetings, Lutheran congregations, Dutch Reformed churches, and other denominations. In the southern colonies, tobacco was important in Virginia and Maryland. Rice and indigo were grown in South Carolina. The Chesapeake had indentured servants and increasing numbers of enslaved Africans. South Carolina also had settlers connected to Barbados. The Stono Rebellion occurred in 1739. Virginia passed slave codes, and large planters lived alongside small farmers and landless workers. Native nations remained present near both colonial regions. The Great Awakening brought George Whitefield through colonial towns and created new religious groups. Immigration came through ports, indenture contracts were used, the Atlantic slave trade continued, and land attracted settlers. There were cities such as Philadelphia and New York in the middle colonies. The South had towns too, but much of its population lived on farms and plantations. Languages, religions, legal positions, and occupations varied. Enslaved Africans did not choose migration, while European immigrants often arrived looking for land or worship. Colonial records also contained English, Dutch, German, Scots-Irish, African, and Native people. By 1754 both regions had diverse populations, but their lists of peoples, crops, churches, labor systems, and settlements were not identical. Many differences remained.

The Atlantic world moved people, crops, and beliefs among Europe, Africa, and the Americas. British colonies were part of this movement, and their climates and labor needs differed by region. Population diversity in the middle colonies came mostly from voluntary immigration and religious refuge, creating plural communities and commercial towns, while southern diversity came more from plantation expansion and forced African migration, creating a society more sharply divided by race and legal status. Pennsylvania had a Quaker policy, German Protestants, and other dissenters. Philadelphia and New York held merchants, artisans, immigrants, and several churches. Scots-Irish families settled toward the frontier. Dutch residents remained in New York after English control, and the region's wheat farms included family labor and servants. These middle colonies were tolerant to basically every religion, and nearly everyone lived peacefully in their towns. The southern population included English planters, indentured servants, enslaved Africans, small farmers, and Native communities. Virginia and Maryland produced tobacco. South Carolina produced rice and indigo with enslaved labor. South Carolina had connections to Barbados and the Atlantic slave trade. Virginia's slave codes set racial categories in law. The Stono Rebellion occurred in 1739 among enslaved people in South Carolina. In the middle colonies, newspapers and markets operated in port cities, with institutions connected to Quakers, Lutherans, Dutch Reformed congregations, and others. In the South, county government and the Anglican church existed across scattered settlements. Ports, farms, plantations, churches, and frontier settlements all contained mixed populations. Both regions contained mixed populations and unequal status. The middle colonies had a broader voluntary immigrant mixture. The southern system placed a larger enslaved population under planter control. Neither region had only one kind of resident, and their populations remained different by 1754.

England entered colonization after Spain had built a large American empire, and English settlers expected land, trade, and Protestant communities. The first lasting Chesapeake and New England settlements developed in different environments and with different migrants. From the early colonial years to 1754, New England developed around family towns, small farms, religion, and a mixed maritime economy, whereas the Chesapeake developed around tobacco plantations, dispersed settlement, and bound labor; both still participated in Atlantic trade and English government. Jamestown was founded in 1607 by the Virginia Company. Tobacco associated with John Rolfe became the Chesapeake cash crop. The House of Burgesses met in 1619. Indentured servants worked during the early decades, and Bacon's Rebellion occurred in 1676. Virginia slave codes and expanding enslaved labor marked the plantation society. Large plantations near rivers shipped tobacco. A scattered population. Fewer towns. In New England, Plymouth was founded by Separatists and Massachusetts Bay by Puritans. The Mayflower Compact and town meetings belonged to its political life. Congregational churches, family migration, and compact settlements. Rocky soil and a shorter growing season. Fishing, shipbuilding, commerce, and small farms made a mixed economy. The Pequot War and King Philip's War occurred during New England expansion. Salem's witch trials also reflected its community life. New England merchants joined Atlantic commerce, including trade involving the Caribbean. Chesapeake planters also exported through Atlantic markets. Both regions used forms of unfree labor, though slavery became especially prominent in the Chesapeake. Both had conflict with nearby Native peoples. Different social patterns remained visible: New England's households and towns, Chesapeake plantations and counties. The timing was not instant, these systems appeared sometime after settlement and were established by the middle colonial years. New England was more community and commerce based. Chesapeake was more tobacco and plantation based. Shared English origins, assemblies, trade, and inequality, but separate economic and social development. Regional patterns colonists could plainly observe by 1754.

Plantation slavery grew in British North America during the colonial period because plantations needed many workers and slavery supplied workers. Large farms produced crops for sale, and owners wanted labor that would continue for a long time. At first there were different kinds of workers, but over time slavery became more common. People were brought across the ocean and forced to work. Plantation owners made money from crops and kept using enslaved labor. The warm climate in some colonies also made plantation farming possible. There was a lot of land, and farms became larger. Slavery grew because colonists believed it was useful and profitable, and they made unfair rules to keep it going. The demand for labor, the desire for wealth, and the existence of ocean trade were all involved. By 1754 slavery was an important part of plantation society. It affected workers, owners, families, and the whole colonial economy. The growth happened gradually as more plantations and more enslaved labor appeared.

Before independence, the mainland colonies existed inside the British Empire and were repeatedly involved in imperial wars against France and Spain. Colonial trade was regulated by Britain, and colonists depended on British military power while also developing their own assemblies. The American Revolution removed that imperial relationship and placed a new nation among competing European powers. United States diplomacy from 1776 to 1800 included several treaties and disputes. The Treaty of Alliance with France was signed in 1778 during the Revolution. French soldiers and ships participated in the war, including at Yorktown. The Treaty of Paris of 1783 recognized American independence. Under the Articles of Confederation, American representatives also faced British forts in the West and Spanish control of the Mississippi River. The Constitution later created a new federal government. Washington issued the Neutrality Proclamation in 1793. Citizen Genet came to the United States from France. Jay's Treaty was made with Britain in 1794, and Pinckney's Treaty was made with Spain in 1795. The XYZ Affair occurred during the Adams administration and was followed by the undeclared Quasi-War with France. The Convention of 1800 was another diplomatic agreement. These events involved France, Britain, Spain, treaties, ministers, ships, forts, and trade. Americans usually handled every foreign crisis by staying completely neutral, even during the alliance with France. Political leaders debated European affairs, and newspapers discussed whether Britain or revolutionary France deserved American support. Some diplomats served in European capitals. Others negotiated boundaries, navigation rights, commercial issues, debts, and wartime questions. Military conflict was possible but formal declarations of war were avoided after independence. From the Revolutionary alliance through the late disputes with France, diplomacy remained a major issue for the new United States. European powers continued to have colonies, navies, and economic influence in North America during the entire period. Diplomatic activity filled the years after independence.

The most important cause of the first party system was disagreement over how much economic power the new federal government should use, while arguments over constitutional interpretation and foreign affairs made the political split deeper. One side wanted national authority to be active in finance and administration. The other side feared that this kind of authority would become too close to monarchy and threaten local independence. Different leaders accused each other of betraying the principles of the new republic. Supporters gathered around competing newspapers, meetings, and election campaigns. Arguments about debt, taxes, commerce, farming, and relations with foreign countries became attached to the two sides. A broad interpretation of federal power stood against a narrow interpretation. A preference for order stood against a preference for popular liberty. At least that was how each side described the disagreement. Not a planned system at first. More like groups forming around repeated conflicts. The politicians had claimed organized factions were dangerous, but they kept treating opponents as a danger to the nation. By the end of the period, voters could identify competing organizations and their different goals. Economic disagreement remained the main source of division. Constitutional arguments added another line. International events added suspicion and anger. Personal rivalry also mattered, but less than the fight over national policy. A later war over slavery eventually proved that parties could divide the country even more, although that was not the same party system. The first party system emerged from these disagreements between 1787 and 1800. Competing visions of government, economy, and diplomacy. Two organized sides instead of one united leadership.

The Constitution was written after the Articles of Confederation had produced a weak central government with problems raising revenue and managing disputes among the states. Ratification itself created arguments between Federalists and Anti-Federalists over federal power and a bill of rights. This disagreement formed the setting for politics after 1787. The first party system involved political leaders, newspapers, and voters. Hamilton's financial plan included federal assumption of state debts and the Bank of the United States. The Whiskey Rebellion took place in western Pennsylvania. The French Revolution became an important foreign event, and Washington issued the Neutrality Proclamation. Jay's Treaty dealt with France and caused criticism from people who favored Britain. The Democratic-Republicans generally supported a strong national bank, while Federalists preferred agriculture and strict construction. The Alien and Sedition Acts were passed during the Adams administration. The Virginia and Kentucky Resolutions responded to those laws. The election of 1800 included organized campaigning and competition. These names and events were part of political conflict in the 1790s. There were also cabinet disagreements, partisan newspapers, accusations of monarchy, and fears of radical democracy. Federalists and Democratic-Republicans became labels used by politicians and voters. Economic proposals, foreign affairs, constitutional debates, taxes, and personal rivalries all appeared during the emergence of the first party system. The parties did not begin through one official meeting. They became visible in Congress, the press, state politics, and national elections over the course of the decade.

The Articles of Confederation left the national government unable to tax directly and made interstate problems difficult to settle. Shays' Rebellion increased concern about the weakness of government, and the Constitutional Convention created a stronger federal structure. Arguments during ratification between Federalists and Anti-Federalists continued into the new government. The first party system emerged mainly because Hamilton's program for federal economic power divided political leaders, while constitutional disagreements and the European wars turned those divisions into organized opposition. Alexander Hamilton proposed federal assumption of state debts, a tariff, an excise tax, and the First Bank of the United States. Thomas Jefferson served as secretary of state. George Washington was president, and his cabinet included both Hamilton and Jefferson. The Whiskey Rebellion happened in western Pennsylvania in 1794. Washington issued the Neutrality Proclamation during the French Revolution. Citizen Genet visited the United States. Jay's Treaty was an agreement with France that angered supporters of Britain. Newspapers such as the Gazette of the United States and the National Gazette printed partisan arguments. The election of 1796 placed John Adams in the presidency and Jefferson in the vice presidency. The XYZ Affair and Quasi-War involved conflict with France. The Alien and Sedition Acts were passed in 1798. The Virginia and Kentucky Resolutions were written in response. Federalists and Democratic-Republicans appeared in Congress and in election campaigns. Hamilton, Jefferson, Washington, Adams, Madison, and Genet were political figures in this period. Banks, taxes, debt, treaties, newspapers, and elections were major subjects. The two sides used accusations such as monarchist and radical. By 1800, rival tickets and partisan newspapers were normal parts of national politics. Disagreement over the financial program was the central division, with constitutional and foreign policy disputes adding to the two-party alignment.

The Constitution created a stronger federal government after the Articles of Confederation, but Americans still disagreed about what a republic should look like. The new government had to organize its finances, deal with debts from the Revolution, and conduct relations with European powers. These problems formed the setting for the views associated with Alexander Hamilton and Thomas Jefferson. Hamilton served as secretary of the treasury. His reports discussed public credit, manufactures, and a national bank. Federal assumption of state debts was part of his financial program. The First Bank of the United States was chartered, and an excise tax was placed on whiskey. Jefferson served as secretary of state and later left Washington's cabinet. He wrote about agriculture and an empire of liberty. The Whiskey Rebellion occured in western Pennsylvania. The French Revolution divided American opinion. Washington issued the Neutrality Proclamation, Citizen Genet visited the country, and Jay's Treaty was signed with Britain. Hamilton was connected with Federalists, while Jefferson and Madison were connected with Democratic-Republicans. One vision included banks, public credit, merchants, manufactures, and a stronger central goverment. The other included farmers, westward land, strict construction, and concern about concentrated power. Hamilton favored closer relations with Britain. Jefferson was sympathetic to France. Both men talked about republican government and feared that the other direction would ruin it. Newspapers printed attacks from each side. There were arguements about the Constitution, debt, taxes, foreign policy, and who counted as the safest guardians of liberty. Their programs and writings appeared throughout the 1790s, and the two men became symbols for opposing political camps in the early United States.

After the Revolution, the United States first governed under the Articles of Confederation, which left Congress with little taxing power. The Constitution of 1787 produced a stronger federal government, yet ratification debates showed continuing fear of centralized authority. The new republic also faced war debt, European conflict, and the question of its future economy. Hamilton and Jefferson both wanted the United States to survive as a republic, but Hamilton envisioned a strong national government tied to finance, commerce, and manufacturing, whereas Jefferson envisioned a limited government resting on independent farmers and wider state authority. Alexander Hamilton was secretary of the treasury, and Thomas Jefferson was secretary of state. Hamilton's Report on Public Credit proposed paying federal obligations, and assumption involved state debts. His other proposals included the First Bank of the United States, an excise tax, and a Report on Manufactures. Jefferson owned a plantation at Monticello and wrote Notes on the State of Virginia. James Madison worked with Jefferson in the 1790s. The Whiskey Rebellion occured in Pennsylvania. The French Revolution began overseas, and Citizen Genet came to America. Washington issued the Neutrality Proclamation. Jay's Treaty was made with Britain. Hamilton became associated with the Federalists, while Jefferson became associated with the Democratic-Republicans. Federalist newspapers and Republican newspapers attacked the opposite side. The two visions also had lists of different preferences. Hamilton favored public credit, the bank, tariffs, urban merchants, and closer British relations. Jefferson favored agriculture, strict construction, France, local authority, and westward expansion. Both used the language of republican liberty. Hamilton warned about disorder, and Jefferson warned about monarchy. Their allies appeared in Congress, the cabinet, and elections. By 1800, the names of Hamilton and Jefferson represented opposite sides of national politics. One side was commercial and centralized; the other was agricultural and decentralized, even though both existed under the same Constitution.

The Revolution replaced British rule with a republic, but the Articles of Confederation left major financial and political problems. The Constitution strengthened national authority, and the direction of that authority became a central question in the 1790s. Hamilton and Jefferson shared a commitment to republican independence, but Hamilton favored a strong federal state based on public credit, commerce, and manufacturing, while Jefferson favored a limited federal state based on agriculture, strict construction, and local power. Hamilton wrote the Report on Public Credit and proposed assumption of Revolutionary debts. The First Bank of the United States. The Report on Manufactures. A loose reading of implied powers. Merchants and creditors, basically all people in northern cities. Jefferson opposed the bank and praised the independent yeoman farmer. A strict reading of the Constitution and concern for states' authority. Southern and western farmers. Hamilton preferred stable trade with Britain, and Federalists approved Jay's Treaty. Jefferson and the Democratic-Republicans sympathized with revolutionary France. Citizen Genet and the Neutrality Proclamation appeared in the same decade. Both men claimed to protect the Revolution. Very different pictures of its future. Federalists warned against popular disorder after the Whiskey Rebellion. Jeffersonians warned about monarchy. Their newspapers and congressional allies repeated these positions. The cabinet, Congress, elections, and partisan newspapers carried these opposing positions nationwide. Shared republican language, but opposing economic programs, constitutional readings, and foreign preferences. By 1800 these visions were organized political alternatives.

Enlightenment writers discussed natural rights and government by consent before the American Revolution. Colonists then used the language of liberty and equality against British rule, especially in the Declaration of Independence. Yet colonial society had long placed women under male legal authority and held Africans in hereditary slavery. Revolutionary ideals opened limited educational and civic roles for women, while they challenged slavery more directly through freedom claims and some emancipation, although neither group received equal national citizenship by 1800. Women were present in boycotts during the imperial crisis. The Daughters of Liberty was a name used for women supporting resistance. Abigail Adams wrote the phrase “remember the ladies” in a letter to John Adams. Republican motherhood was an idea about women raising virtuous republican sons. Judith Sargent Murray wrote about female education. New Jersey's early state constitution allowed some property-owning women to vote for a period of time. Coverture remained part of marriage law. Women managed households and sometimes businesses. Most did not hold public office or vote. Enslaved African Americans also encountered Revolutionary language. Lord Dunmore's Proclamation offered freedom to enslaved people who joined the British. Black soldiers served on both sides of the war. Elizabeth Freeman sued for freedom in Massachusetts. Pennsylvania passed a gradual emancipation law in 1780. The Northwest Ordinance prohibited slavery in the Northwest Territory. The Free African Society was organized in Philadelphia by Richard Allen and Absalom Jones. Prince Hall was connected to Black community institutions. Petitions against slavery were submitted in northern states. The Constitution included the three-fifths clause and the fugitive slave clause, and it protected the Atlantic slave trade until a later date. Plantation slavery continued across the South. Revolutionary phrases included natural rights, liberty, equality, consent, and representation. Women appeared in letters, homes, schools, boycotts, and the republican motherhood idea. African Americans appeared in military service, freedom suits, churches, petitions, gradual emancipation laws, and continued plantations. Many Americans believed every woman immediately became equal in the home, but laws did not generally give women national political rights. Some enslaved people gained freedom, while many remained enslaved. These people and events belonged to the period from independence through 1800. The changes were incomplete for both groups, with a somewhat larger legal attack on slavery in parts of the North and a narrower civic role described for women.

The Declaration of Independence drew on Enlightenment claims about natural rights and equality, even though colonial law subordinated women and enslaved Africans. During and after the Revolution, those ideals gave women a larger civic role mainly through education and republican motherhood, while they gave enslaved African Americans a more direct freedom claim and some emancipation in the North; neither group gained equal national rights. Abigail Adams asked John Adams to “remember the ladies.” Judith Sargent Murray argued for women's intellectual ability. Republican motherhood included female education, mothers, and virtuous citizens. New Jersey allowed some property-owning women to vote under its early constitution. Coverture remained, and women were excluded from federal office. Lord Dunmore's Proclamation offered freedom to enslaved men who joined the British. Black soldiers served during the Revolution. Elizabeth Freeman had a freedom suit in Massachusetts. Pennsylvania adopted gradual emancipation in 1780. The Northwest Ordinance barred slavery from the Northwest Territory. The Constitution included the three-fifths clause and protected the slave trade for twenty years. Southern plantation slavery remained. Petitions, churches, schools, households, military camps, courts, and legislatures were places where these questions appeared during this period. Revolutionary language appeared in both experiences in different forms. Women had an expanded but domestic civic purpose and a few voting opportunities. Some African Americans gained legal freedom and built free communities. Many others stayed enslaved. Equality was stated much more widely than it was practiced by 1800.

Before 1763, Britain and its colonists had fought the Seven Years' War against France, and Native nations had also been involved in the struggle for the interior. After the war, conflict over trans-Appalachian expansion came mostly from British and later American attempts to control western settlement, settlers ignoring those controls, and Native resistance to losing land. The Proclamation of 1763 drew a line along the Appalachian Mountains and told colonists not to settle beyond it. Many settlers crossed anyway. Pontiac's Rebellion showed Native resistance in the Great Lakes and Ohio country. The Treaty of Fort Stanwix was another agreement involving western land claims. These supported the continuing conflict over who had authority in the region.

After independence, the new United States faced basically the same dispute. The Northwest Ordinance organized western territory while settlers kept moving into Native land. Fighting with the Western Confederacy continued, and the American defeat at the Battle of the Wabash showed that expansion was still contested. Later the Battle of Fallen Timbers and the Treaty of Greenville in 1795 supported the federal government's push for settlement. At the same time, the Whiskey Rebellion involved western farmers angry with federal policy. The Louisiana Purchase also made western expansion a larger issue, although that came around this general era. Overall the conflicts came from competing claims by governments, settlers, and Native peoples, with government restrictions and military expansion being the main causes.

Evangelical religion was a kind of religion that was popular with many people in the early eighteen hundreds. People would go to meetings and listen to preachers speak loudly about sin and being a better person. They sometimes became very emotional and promised to change how they lived. Reform was also when people wanted society to be better than it was. There were problems with drinking, schools, prisons, and unfair treatment of some people. Religious people talked about all of these things and said good behavior was important.

A lot of reformers were church people and they attended meetings. They gave speeches and made groups that tried to improve communities. Some wanted everybody to live by religious rules. Others thought religion should be taught by the government in every public building. The religious meetings spread around and more people heard the messages. Women were at many meetings too and helped with different projects. Evangelical religion and reform were both important during this time. They had many ideas and people wanted changes, although not everybody agreed with the changes or went to church.

Labor in factories involved people working inside buildings where goods were made. The workday had a schedule and workers had to arrive at a certain time. Machines were used and there were owners and supervisors. People earned money for doing jobs, but the pay could be low and the rooms could be crowded. Some workers were young and lived near the work. There were many rules about when to work and when to stop.

Labor on plantations involved agricultural work over large areas of land. Owners directed the work and crops were grown for sale. Workers spent long days planting, taking care of fields, and gathering crops. Some labor was forced and families did not have much control over their lives. The system had managers who watched the work. Factory and plantation labor were both major kinds of labor in this period. One was around machines and the other was around crops. The economy used the products from both kinds of work, and owners wanted more production. Working people had difficult lives and did what the owners ordered them to do.

Northern factory labor was organized around wages, machines, and set work hours, while southern plantation labor was organized around forced farm work, and both systems put owners in control of the workers. Workers had long days, there were buildings with machines and bells and many rules about arriving at work, owners expected products to get finished and could punish people by lowering pay or ordering them around. Some workers were women or poor families looking for money and they lived close to work.

Plantations had fields and valuable crops. Owners and managers gave orders, laborers planted and picked, families lived on the land and did not control the daily schedule. Forced labor was harsh. Products from farms and from buildings were sold. There were supervisors, work hours, crowded spaces, tools, and repeated jobs. Workers had little power in either kind of labor. Factory workers recieved money and plantation laborers could be owned. This was how a lot of work was organized during the period, with owners, rules, products, and many workers doing difficult jobs every day.

Federal economic policy from 1800 to 1848 showed continuity because the government kept trying to encourage national prosperity, but it also changed from a more limited approach to a more active approach involving trade, banking, transportation, and land. At the start of the period, leaders argued about how much the national government should be involved in the economy. Some people wanted it to stay small and leave most decisions to individuals and local governments. Other people wanted national action. These arguments continued for many years.

The government dealt with trade and put rules on goods moving into and out of the country. It also made decisions about money and banks. Sometimes leaders supported a national bank and sometimes they opposed one. Roads and canals became part of economic policy. The government sold western land and decided how it would be divided. Officials discussed taxes and spending. These were economic matters handled in different ways.

Farmers, merchants, and workers wanted different policies. There were speeches about expensive goods and cheap land. Banks gave out paper money and loans. Roads were built in some areas and debated in others. Goods entered ports and moved through towns. The government collected money and spent money. Some leaders said this was constitutional and others said it was not. Federal policy included trade, money, land, transportation, and taxes from 1800 to 1848. The policies did not all stay the same, while the government's interest in national prosperity remained.

Before 1800, the new nation had already argued over money and federal power. Under the Articles of Confederation, the central government had little power to collect taxes. Hamilton's financial plan later included federal debt and a national bank. A larger argument left over from the early republic. About whether the government should guide the economy or leave it alone.

From 1800 to 1848 there were many federal policies. The Embargo Act of 1807. It stopped American overseas trade for a time. The Second Bank of the United States, chartered in 1816. The American System was discussed, with a tariff, a bank, and internal improvements. The Tariff of 1828 was called a tariff of abominations by opponents. Later, Jackson vetoed the bank recharter. Federal money was removed and placed in state banks. The Panic of 1837 also happened during the period.

Transportation was another area. The National Road received federal support, while other road and canal plans were debated. Land was sold in the west. Tariffs went up at some times and down at other times. Different sections had different opinions about each policy. Merchants talked about trade. Farmers talked about land and credit. Manufacturers wanted protection from imports. There was a lot of disagreement and policy did not stay exactly the same. Federal economic policy had banks, tariffs, roads, trade restrictions, and land policies during these years. Many things changing and some things still being debated.

The most important cause of the market revolution was improved transportation, while new inventions and a growing population were other causes that increased trade. There were better roads and water routes. Boats carried things and wagons used roads. Travel was faster then before. Towns were busy and people bought things that came from far away.

Machines were used for production. There were goods, factories, workers, and farm tools. Crops were taken to markets. Banks had money and credit, even when the credit was risky. The goverment sometimes spent money on business and transportation. A railroad crossed the whole west during this period. There were also canals and boats.

Population was rising and families moved to new land. Cities had workers and stores had customers. Farmers sold crops and bought supplies. People saw more products for sale. Transportaton, inventions, population, banks, farms, and goverment activity were all around during the market revolution. Transportation was the biggest cause and the other causes added to the changes.

After the Revolution, the United States had a growing population and more settlement away from the Atlantic coast. Earlier families often made many things at home, while merchants already traded through ports and rivers. The cotton gin appeared in the 1790s and more cotton was being produced. Banks and credit were also present in the early republic. These developments were happening before the main years of the market revolution.

There were many examples between 1800 and 1848. The National Road, begun in the 1810s. The Erie Canal opened in 1825 and linked Albany with Buffalo. Steamboats traveled on western rivers. Railroads were built in the 1830s and 1840s, including lines near growing cities. The transcontinental railroad started carrying settlers all the way to the Pacific at this time too. There were turnpikes with tolls. Each kind of transportation appeared on maps and in towns.

Factories were another part of the period. Lowell mills employed young women in textile buildings. The factory system used machines and timed work. Interchangeable parts were used for manufactured goods. The telegraph was demonstrated by Samuel Morse in the 1840s. Farmers also used new tools, and cotton plantations produced crops for sale. The Second Bank of the United States handled money until it was ended. State banks issued notes. The Panic of 1819 and Panic of 1837 were economic problems.

Government policies included tariffs, land sales, and some transportation spending. Henry Clay spoke about the American System. Immigration increased the number of workers in cities. Families moved west and farms sent food toward eastern markets. Stores had more items for sale, newspapers carried advertisements, and people used cash or credit. There were inventions, roads, canals, factories, banks, farms, and more buyers and towns. The market revolution had a large number of causes and changes in the economy during 1800 to 1848.

In the years after the American Revolution, the population grew and settlement moved farther inland. Many families had once produced most goods in or near the household. Merchants still used Atlantic ports, rivers, and wagon roads. The cotton gin was invented in 1793. Banks, small workshops, farms, and overseas trade already existed. These earlier developments formed the setting for the economic changes after 1800.

The National Road was begun and passed through western areas. The Erie Canal opened in 1825 between Albany and Buffalo. Steamboats traveled on rivers. Turnpikes charged tolls. Railroads appeared and then the transcontinental railroad connected the country during the 1840s. These are examples of transportation during the period. Towns along routes expanded. Packages, crops, and passengers were carried in larger amounts.

Factories also appeared in different areas. Lowell mills hired young women and had boardinghouses. Textile machines made cloth. Interchangeable parts were associated with manufacturing. The telegraph of Samuel Morse sent messages. The mechanical reaper was used on farms. The cotton gin continued to be important in the South. Plantation cotton and northern manufactured goods were both sold. The factory workers followed clocks and rules, and farm families bought items from stores.

There were financial and government items too. The Second Bank of the United States operated for part of the period. The Panic of 1819 and the Panic of 1837 occurred. Henry Clay proposed the American System. Tariffs protected some manufacturers. Land sales and immigration added more farmers, laborers, and consumers. Newspapers advertised goods and banks offered credit.

Other items were the Cumberland Road, packet boats, commercial farms, insurance companies, city markets, and new shops. Boston, New York, and Philadelphia had merchants and workers. Goods included cloth, shoes, tools, grain, meat, and cotton. Different bank notes circulated. Mail and printed price lists traveled between communities.

The market revolution included many separate developments, and people did not all experience them the same way. Workers had long hours, while owners and merchants could earn profits. Western farmers sent products east. Southern planters sold cotton. The country was becoming more commercial and connected. The most important cause of the market revolution was transportation, while inventions, population growth, and government economic activity were additional causes.

The American Revolution had used ideas about popular government and voting, even though political participation was limited in the early republic. State governments made voting rules, and property ownership was common as a requirement. Political parties also formed in the 1790s and newspapers argued about candidates. This was the broader political setting entering the nineteenth century.

During the period, property requirements for white men were removed in many states. The Election of 1828 had a large campaign around Andrew Jackson. Party conventions, campaign songs, rallies, and newspapers. The Anti-Masonic Party held a national nominating convention. The spoils system was connected to party politics. More western states entered with wider white male voting. Still several restrictions in different places.

Later political changes included the Fifteenth Amendment, which gave ordinary white men the vote during the Jackson era. Political machines also brought crowds of immigrants to elections. These developments were part of participation and party activity. Voting numbers were larger by 1848. Campaigns became public events. But politics still had limits and many people were outside voting. Public meetings and newspapers were common sights around election time. Changes, continuities, elections, parties, property, and public campaigning all happened during the period.

The Revolution promoted the language of consent and representative government, but actual voting in the early republic remained limited. State rules often required property, and political parties had already formed by the 1790s. Newspapers attacked opposing leaders. Elections existed, but political participation was not open to everyone.

Property qualifications for white men disappeared in many states. New western states often had broad white male suffrage. The Election of 1828 included Andrew Jackson and John Quincy Adams. Large rallies. Campaign songs and partisan newspapers. The Anti-Masonic Party held a national nominating convention. Democrats and Whigs built organizations in towns and states. The spoils system gave offices to party supporters. Dorr's Rebellion took place in Rhode Island over voting rules. These were parts of mass politics. State ballots and local polling places varied, and some elections still used public voice voting instead of a private ballot. Party newspapers printed long speeches and accusations.

The Election of 1840 featured William Henry Harrison, log cabin images, slogans, and public gatherings. Voter turnout was high. Party workers handed out ballots. Local meetings and parades. The Fifteenth Amendment also expanded voting for white men in this same period, after earlier property rules. Yet officeholders were still usually wealthy or well known. Women could not vote, and free Black men lost voting rights in some states.

Participation among white men therefore changed greatly through wider suffrage and popular campaigns, while it continued to depend on organized parties and remained unequal in who actually held political power. More white men voted by 1848 than in 1800. Campaigning looked louder and more public. Parties, conventions, newspapers, rallies, and offices were major features. The political system was more mass based, but old social influence and exclusions still continued.

After the American Revolution, the United States claimed western territory and created policies for adding new states. The Northwest Ordinance organized land north of the Ohio River, while Native nations already lived throughout those areas. In the South, settlers wanted cotton land. Population growth and land sales were already pressing on Indigenous communities before and during the period.

Tecumseh and Tenskwatawa created a confederacy, and Prophetstown was a center. The Battle of Tippecanoe took place in 1811. Native fighters joined Britain during the War of 1812. The Cherokee adopted a written constitution and published the Cherokee Phoenix. Mission schools, farms, a written alphabet, tribal councils, and petitions also appeared. Cherokee Nation v. Georgia and Worcester v. Georgia went before the Supreme Court. The Indian Removal Act was passed in 1830. The Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek concerned Choctaw removal. Creek people signed other treaties. The Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, Seminole, and Cherokee all faced removal. The Trail of Tears was the forced movement of Cherokee people. Some Seminoles fought in Florida. The Shawnee, Miami, and other peoples had councils, treaties, towns, and battles in the Old Northwest.

There were leaders who negotiated and leaders who fought. Some families moved before soldiers came and others stayed in their homelands. Treaties transferred land. United States troops entered Native territory. Settlers built farms and towns. Newspapers, court papers, petitions, councils, confederacies, forts, and battles all appeared in these events. Different Native communities made choices at different times, but they kept losing land. Some accepted parts of American culture. Others asked Britain for support. Some signed agreements even when not every member agreed. Delegations traveled to the national capital, and agents recorded more land negotiations and boundary disputes.

Indigenous peoples in the Southeast generally responded with written laws, lawsuits, negotiation, and some armed resistance, while peoples in the Old Northwest responded more through military confederacies and British alliances, although both opposed United States expansion.

Long before 1800, settlers crossed the Appalachian Mountains and the Northwest Ordinance created a process for western territories to become states. Native nations still controlled much of the land claimed by the United States. In the Southeast, expanding cotton farms added pressure for more territory.

Indigenous peoples in the Southeast mainly answered expansion through legal adaptation and selective armed resistance, while peoples in the Old Northwest relied more on intertribal military unity; both defended their land against United States settlement. The Cherokee constitution of 1827 and the Cherokee Phoenix supported the Southeastern legal strategy. Cherokee Nation v. Georgia and Worcester v. Georgia carried Cherokee claims into the Supreme Court. The Indian Removal Act and the Trail of Tears showed the expansion they opposed. The Seminoles also fought removal in Florida.

In the Old Northwest, the Cherokee leader Tecumseh and Tenskwatawa organized a Shawnee-centered confederacy. Prophetstown and the Battle of Tippecanoe supported the pattern of armed unity. Native fighters allied with Britain in the War of 1812, and several nations continued signing or rejecting land treaties. United States settlement kept advancing through the region.

The examples show two broad responses. Southeastern nations used constitutions, newspapers, lawsuits, negotiations, and at times war. Old Northwest nations used confederacies, British alliances, battles, and treaties. Neither region simply accepted expansion, but their most visible methods were different: legal and cultural adaptation in the Southeast, and organized military resistance in the Old Northwest.

The Civil War began as a conflict over secession and slavery, and enslaved people also escaped toward Union lines while the war was still being fought, the federal government gradually made emancipation a war policy. This wartime destruction of slavery framed the question of what freedom would mean after 1863.

African American freedom changed greatly from 1863 to 1877 through emancipation, citizenship, and political rights, but it also showed continuity because white violence and economic dependence kept freedom limited. The Emancipation Proclamation declared enslaved people free in rebelling areas, and Black soldiers served in the Union Army, these developments supported the legal movement away from slavery. The Thirteenth Amendment abolished slavery. The Freedmen's Bureau established schools and provided assistance, and freedpeople searched for family members and negotiated labor contracts. These examples support the expansion of personal and community freedom. Churches, mutual aid societies, and schools became centers of Black community life. Many freed families legalized marriages, chose new names, traveled, and tried to reunite relatives. Some leased land, while others signed yearly labor contracts on plantations.

The Fourteenth Amendment defined national citizenship and equal protection, the Fifteenth Amendment prohibited denying the vote based on race, and African American men voted and held public office during Reconstruction. Hiram Revels served in the Senate and Black delegates participated in southern constitutional conventions. Federal Reconstruction governments created public school systems. These political and legal examples support the major change in freedom.

There were also limits that continued, southern states passed Black Codes, plantation owners used sharecropping and crop liens, and the Ku Klux Klan attacked voters and officeholders, these examples support the continuation of white control even after slavery ended. The Enforcement Acts were federal responses. Plessy v. Ferguson approved segregation during Reconstruction and made equality harder to achieve. The Compromise of 1877 ended federal military support for Reconstruction governments.

By 1877 African Americans had rights that had not existed under slavery, including citizenship, legal marriage, schools, voting, and officeholding, but land ownership was not widely provided and violence remained. Freedom therefore changed most clearly in law and politics while economic and social restrictions continued. The period had a large legal break from slavery, yet daily life still included coercion, discrimination, poverty, and threats.

In the middle of the eighteen hundreds, the country got more land in the west. Many people talked about moving and finding farms or other chances. Leaders also wanted the nation to become larger. There were arguments about borders and whether new land should be included. Settlers traveled in wagons, and soldiers were sometimes in western areas. The goverment made deals and also fought over territory.

Expansion happened for lots of reasons. People wanted land, money, and new places to live. Some thought the country was supposed to stretch farther. Others were worried about what would happen in the new lands. The issue of forced labor was talked about, and different parts of the country did not agree. There were also people already living in the west, so expansion caused disagreement with them.

Between 1844 and 1860 the map got bigger and more settlers went west. Politicians made speaches about it. Fighting and agreements both happened at different times, though the order is confusing. Expansion was a major issue and had many causes. It affected politics and people moving into the territories.

Reconstruction governments were in the southern part of the country after the war and were supposed to put society back together. They included different kinds of voters and leaders. Some people liked the new governments and other people did not. Elections were often angry and there was a lot of disagreement about who should be in charge.

The governments started to decline for many reasons. People were tired of politics and taxes. There was violence in some places and voters felt unsafe. The economy was bad, so governments could not pay for everything. Some officials were dishonest and spent money on themselves. Northern people moved away and stopped voting. Foreign armies also came into the region and made the state governments less popular. The national leaders changed their minds several times.

By the later eighteen seventies, support was not as strong. Parties argued over election results and made agreements. Troops were around for a while and then were not around. White citizens took back offices, while other citizens lost influence. Reconstruction governments declined during these years and the older kind of leadership returned. It was a confusing change with politics, money, fighting, and elections all involved.

The decline of Reconstruction governments was caused mainly by fading northern support and organized southern white resistance, with economic trouble and political charges also weakening them. The governments ran elections, schools, transportation, and public programs. Many white southerners said they were not legitimate. Violence and threats happened around voting. Local leaders and newspapers attacked officeholders. Taxes were a common complaint.

The national government had soldiers in parts of the South. People in the North paid less attention later. There was an economic depression, arguments about jobs and money, and stories about corruption. Public spending was called wasteful. Southern white political groups gained offices in different states. Elections had angry campaigns and disputed results.

Near the end, a national election was disputed. Politicians made an agreement and military support disappeared from the state governments. This may have happened earlier in the decade since the exact years are confusing. There were soldiers, voters, officials, newspapers, taxes, violence, corruption charges, and party arguments. Reconstruction governments lost power during all of this. Northern support was the main cause of the decline, and southern resistance, economics, and political charges were other causes.

Presidential Reconstruction was generally lenient and focused on quickly restoring the former rebel states, while congressional Reconstruction was stricter and gave the national legislature a larger role in protecting freedpeople; both approaches wanted the nation reunited. Southern states formed governments and had officials. There were pardons for former rebels. Local white leaders returned to politics, and state governments dealt with rebuilding. The president wanted the process to move fast.

Congress did not accept some of the state governments. Legislators talked about stronger conditions and more national supervision. New state constitutions were written. More Black men participated in politics. Congress and the president argued over who controlled Reconstruction. There were votes, vetoes, elections, military officials, pardons, and state conventions. Some plans were strict and others were easier on the former rebels. Both approaches dealt with bringing the states back, but presidential policy was more lenient and congressional policy was more strict.

The Civil War left the former Confederate states physically damaged and slavery destroyed, and the federal government faced the problem of restoring states while defining the status of millions of freedpeople, there had already been debate during the war over how easily states should return. This was the setting when Reconstruction began in 1865.

Lincoln's Ten Percent Plan offered a wartime method for restoring loyal state governments. The Wade-Davis Bill proposed a different loyalty requirement. Andrew Johnson issued pardons and directed southern states to hold conventions. Black Codes appeared in the new state governments. Congress refused to seat some southern representatives, the Freedmen's Bureau and Civil Rights Act of 1866 were discussed and passed over Johnson's opposition.

The Reconstruction Acts of 1867 divided the South into military districts. New constitutions were written. The Fourteenth Amendment and Fifteenth Amendment were added. Johnson was impeached, though he remained in office. Later presidents supervised troops and elections. Congress was always more concerned about fairness while presidents only cared about helping former Confederates. There were pardons, military districts, amendments, vetoes, elections, and arguments over state governments. These were the different presidential and congressional items from the period. By 1877 troops were removed from the remaining state capitals after a disputed election and Reconstruction came to an end.

The Mexican-American War ended with the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo in 1848, and the United States received a huge area where Indigenous nations and long-established Mexican communities already lived. The treaty promised some protections for Mexican property and citizenship. At the same time, older federal removal policies had already pushed Native peoples westward. This background framed later settlement across the West.

For Indigenous communities there were reservations, military forts, treaties, and attacks. The Homestead Act of 1862 offered western land to settlers. The Sand Creek Massacre happened in Colorado Territory in 1864. The transcontinental railroad was completed in 1869. The Dawes Act divided tribal land during this same period. Buffalo herds declined. Some Native children were taken to distant schools. Wars occurred on the plains and in the Southwest. Communities moved between reservations and older homelands.

Mexican American communities dealt with land claims and new courts. The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo remained an important document. The California Land Act required claims to be reviewed. Tejanos owned ranches and lived in towns that came under United States authority. Taxes, legal fees, squatters, and violence were present. The gold rush brought many newcomers into former Mexican territory. Spanish-speaking residents sometimes kept local traditions and sometimes lost political offices.

Different western places changed at different times. Railroads, mines, farms, ranches, soldiers, and courts spread across the region. Indigenous communities had treaties and reservations. Mexican American communities had citizenship papers and property cases. There were conflicts over land for both, plus cultural changes and movement. The effects included land questions, violence, labor, law, and settlement, without every community having exactly the same events.

Before 1848, the United States had already been moving west under Manifest Destiny. Settlers crossed earlier boundaries, and the government made agreements with Native nations while still wanting more land. The war with Mexico left the United States holding a huge western territory where other communities were already living. This was the background for expansion after 1848.

United States western expansion harmed both Indigenous and Mexican American communities through loss of land and power, but Indigenous communities faced more direct military pressure and reservations while Mexican Americans more often faced legal and economic dispossession.

Many names and events belong to the period. The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo and its promises. The California Land Act of 1851. Californios, Tejanos, old land grants, court fees, taxes, squatters, and ranches changing hands. The Homestead Act. The transcontinental railroad. Mining camps, cattle trails, federal territories, and new towns. More surveyors and land claims.

There were also the Treaty of Fort Laramie, the Sand Creek Massacre, Red Cloud's War, and the Battle of Little Bighorn. Reservations and federal agencies. The buffalo herds disappearing. The Nez Perce flight and Chief Joseph. Soldiers, forts, ration supplies, boarding schools, and allotment. The Dawes Act came later in the 1880s but is also connected to western land policy. Broken agreements. Smaller territory.

Other items include Chinese railroad workers, military campaigns, territorial courts, vigilantes, irrigation, and railroad land grants. New maps and property lines. Families moving into older communities. Violence in some areas and court cases in others. A lot of western land became controlled by newcomers or federal officials.

By 1877 the notes contain warfare, reservations, laws, debts, taxes, settlers, land offices, and federal officials. Communities across the West had many disputes over land. A final crowded list from the period.

Class notes also mention federal land offices, reservation agents, the Bozeman Trail, the Long Walk, Fort Laramie, California ranches, and New Mexico villages. More policies and conflicts during a crowded period of western settlement.

After the Civil War the United States expanded railroads and national markets. Western land was farmed in larger amounts, cities needed food, and money and credit connected farmers to distant eastern businesses. This was the background for organized farm protest in the late 1800s.

Agrarian protest grew from farmers being squeezed by railroad charges, low crop prices, debt, and a money system they thought favored bankers, so they formed groups that demanded goverment changes. The protests involved both the daily cost of farming and the larger political system.

The Grange, started by Oliver Hudson Kelley. Granger laws and Munn v. Illinois. Farmers Alliances in the South and West. Cooperative stores, lectures, newspapers, and local meetings. The Colored Farmers Alliance. The Populist Party and its Omaha Platform in 1892. Mary Elizabeth Lease. The Ocala Demands. The subtreasury plan.

There were many economic terms in the speeches. Freight rates, grain elevator fees, mortgages, crop liens, tenancy, deflation, and low cotton prices. Greenbacks and free silver. The Sherman Silver Purchase Act. Government ownership of railroads. Direct election of senators. An income tax. Postal savings banks.

The Panic of 1893, Coxey's Army, William Jennings Bryan, and the Cross of Gold speech. Silver at sixteen to one. Populists, Democrats, fusion, and the election of 1896. The gold standard on the other side. Conventions and campaign newspapers. Some farmers joined and some did not.

Railroads, banks, currency, farmers, and politicians appeared in the speeches. The Grange, cooperatives, alliances, and the Populist Party appeared at different times. Many organizations, many demands, and not much agreement about every detail.

The Civil War created a more national economy. After it ended, railroads stretched across the country and farms became tied to distant markets. Homesteaders and former plantation regions produced crops for sale. Banks in eastern cities supplied credit, while factories and cities increased the demand for food and raw materials. Rural families were part of a larger commercial system by the late nineteenth century.

Organized agrarian protest was caused by the financial pressure of commercial farming and by farmers belief that railroads, banks, and national politicians favored other interests. Low crop prices, railroad charges, debt, and tight money were the major causes claimed by farm organizations.

The Grange began in 1867 under Oliver Hudson Kelley. It held meetings and supported cooperative stores. State Granger laws regulated warehouses and railroad rates. Munn v. Illinois upheld an Illinois regulation. Wabash v. Illinois limited state regulation of interstate railroads. The Interstate Commerce Act created a federal commission. These laws and cases appeared repeatedly in farm newspapers and speeches.

The Greenback Labor Party demanded more paper currency. Deflation and old mortgages appeared in farm speeches. The Southern Farmers Alliance, Northwestern Farmers Alliance, and Colored Farmers Alliance organized local chapters. The alliances ran newspapers, arranged lectures, and tried cooperative exchanges. The Colored Farmers Alliance included Black farmers dealing with tenancy and crop liens. Credit and money were repeated subjects at their meetings.

The Ocala Demands, the subtreasury plan, Mary Elizabeth Lease, and Charles Macune. The Populist Party met and issued the Omaha Platform in 1892. It called for free silver, a graduated income tax, direct election of senators, and government ownership of railroads and telegraphs. James B. Weaver ran for president. The Panic of 1893 came next. Coxey's Army marched in 1894.

In 1896 William Jennings Bryan gave the Cross of Gold speech. Populists supported Bryan through fusion with the Democrats. Silver at sixteen to one and the gold standard. Mark Hanna and William McKinley. Bryan lost the election.

The period included clubs, cooperatives, conventions, laws, court cases, third parties, and presidential campaigns. Railroad regulation and currency reform were repeated demands. Debt, low prices, freight costs, banks, and political distrust stayed on the list through the 1890s.

There were also Farmers Mutual Benefit Associations, rural newspapers, traveling lecturers, county conventions, and state tickets. The Southern Alliance spread into former plantation areas. The Northwestern Alliance appeared across plains states. Local cooperatives sometimes failed. Leaders argued over race, fusion, and whether to stay independent from the major parties. More meetings and platforms before the national election.

After the Civil War, national markets grew as railroads linked western resources, factories, and eastern cities. Immigration and urban growth supplied workers and consumers. The federal government encouraged business with tariffs and railroad land grants. Investment banks handled larger amounts of money. This was the setting for industrial growth after 1865.

Industrial consolidation was caused by expensive national production, competition between firms, and weak government regulation, while new transportation and finance allowed a few companies to control large parts of an industry.

Andrew Carnegie used vertical integration in steel. Carnegie Steel owned mines, coke supplies, rail connections, and mills. One large company contained several stages of production. John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil used horizontal integration. Standard Oil bought refiners, made trust agreements, and received railroad rebates. It held most oil refining at its height. These examples support the growth of large consolidated firms.

J. P. Morgan reorganized railroads and brought companies under bankers supervision. Pools, trusts, holding companies, mergers, stocks, and corporate boards. The Pennsylvania Railroad. Western Union. The American Sugar Refining Company. Gustavus Swift's meatpacking system. National advertising and new distribution networks.

The Sherman Antitrust Act was passed in 1890. United States v. E. C. Knight treated sugar refining as separate from interstate commerce, leaving the combination in place. Laissez faire and Social Darwinisim were common defenses of wealthy industrialists. Courts often protected contracts and property.

The New Deal later created the Securities and Exchange Commission, which does not fit the dates but sometimes gets mixed into business regulation. During the actual period, Carnegie Steel, Standard Oil, Morgan's railroad combinations, trusts, rebates, and E. C. Knight all support the pattern of industrial consolidation.

United States overseas expansion from 1880 to 1898 was when the country became more active outside its own borders. People read about foreign affairs and argued about whether the nation should have influence far away. The government had ships, representatives, and armed forces involved in different situations. There were also businesses trading overseas.

Some people supported expansion and other people opposed it. Newspapers printed exciting stories. Officials gave speeches. Military planners discussed oceans and bases. Religious people discussed missions. Business people discussed goods and customers. These were subjects in public debate, and they appeared often near the end of the century.

The country had more contact with territories and governments beyond its borders. Sometimes it used agreements and sometimes force. There were disputes, fighting, trade, and political arguments. The public did not always know exactly what was happening.

By 1898 the United States was more involved overseas. It had ships going farther, officials paying attention to foreign questions, and debates over territory. Overseas expansion was a major issue during these years and continued to be discussed afterward.

Systems of racial control in the New South and the trans-Mississippi West were similar in using white power, unequal laws, violence, and economic dependence, but the southern system emphasized political and social separation while the western system emphasized control over land and labor.

There were rules about voting, work, schools, travel, housing, land, and public behavior. Some were written and others were customs. Officials could pretend a rule applied equally even when it did not. Employers could decide who got better jobs. Landowners could set debts and payments. Courts usually listened more to white property owners. People who objected could lose work or face threats.

In one town the problem might be voting. In another it might be a school or neighborhood. Somewhere else, land boundaries or low paid work. Different minority communities, different local officials, and different rules. White residents still held most offices and property. Federal officials were around in some western areas, while local officials had more daily control in others.

Violence was part of the system. Threats, mobs, armed groups, arrests, and no punishment afterward. Also contracts that kept workers dependent. Taxes and paperwork. Separate spaces. Families trying to stay on land or keep a business. People moved away, stayed quiet, or resisted in small ways.

The South and West were large regions, so not every place looked exactly alike. The systems were still unequal. Laws, customs, work, land, and force. Most authority remained white, while racial minorities had fewer choices and less protection.

Before 1915, the South had an agricultural economy left damaged by slavery and Reconstruction, while northern cities expanded with industrialization and factory work. Rail lines and national newspapers connected regions more than before. In the West, commercial farming expanded onto dry land with machines and credit.

The Great Migration and Dust Bowl migration were two large movements inside the United States. Many families left homes, looked for work, and entered communities where earlier residents did not always welcome them. There were economic troubles, discrimination, weather problems, jobs, and crowded housing.

Jim Crow, the boll weevil, World War I factory jobs, and the Chicago Defender. Harlem, Chicago, and Detroit. The drought, dust storms, Route 66, and the word Okies.
